title: practical english usage - Part 34
content:
after an auxiliary verb.

In British English (but not American), do can be used alone as a substitute verb auxiliary       verb    + do 28 do:  substitute       verb    (/ may     do.) Be,  have do     Section 2 and grammar •  29  do so/it/that For auxiliary do as substitute for a whole verb phrase, ►    279.   For differences between it and that, ►     145.

For so with think, believe, hope and similar verbs, ►    585.

For so I am, so U is, etc, ►    309.3.   For so do I, so am I, etc, ►    309.1.   For so with say and tell, ►    586.

It is not possible in standard English to say I can so, She was it or I have that.

Note that so, it and that are not normally used in this way after auxiliary verbs.

usually use these expressions to replace verbs like fall, lose, like, remember, Do so/it/that are mainly used to refer to deliberate dynamic actions. use 'tense' for all verb forms which  show time.

In academic grammars, the term 'aspect' is often used for progressive forms. We terminology:        tense     and   aspect Call me if you're passing through London.

I'll meet you when you arrive.

Both present tenses can be used to talk about the future (►  31.4, 36-37).

'What are you doing?' ‘ I'm reading.' I'm travelling a lot these days.

going on now or around now, we  usually use a present progressive tense (►  32).

When we  talk about temporary continuing actions and events, which  are just around      now:   present      progressive Water freezes at 0°  Celsius. My parents live near Dover. I often go swimming.

repeatedly, we  usually use the simple present (►   30-31    for details).

When  we  talk about timeless truths, permanent situations or things that happen general      time:   simple     present thinking are called 'present progressive' or ‘ present continuous'.

called ‘ simple present' or ‘ present simple'; forms like I am waiting or she's Most English verbs have two present tenses. The  pronunciation of the -(e)s ending depends on the sound that comes before 3 pronunciation          of  third    person     singular      forms do -> does go goes Exceptions:                                     have -> has fix -> fixes push -> pushes watch -> watches add -es to infinitive                    buzz -> buzzes Verbs ending in -s, -z, -ch, -sh or -x:    miss -> misses But (vowel + y):           enjoy -> enjoys reply -» replies change y to i and add -es      hurry -> hurries Verbs ending in consonant + y:           cry -> cries stay stays add -s to infinitive                     sit -> sits Most verbs:                                           work -> works 2 spelling      of  third    person     singular      forms For passives (e.g. The  work is done), ►    57.
